solution.p: We will factor r^{2}t^{2}-rt-6 where a=1, b=-1, and c=-6.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.

For r^{2}t^{2}-rt-6, since the product of -3 and 2 is -6, and the sum of -3 and 2 is -1, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}t^{2}-rt-6\amp =\left(rt-3\right)\mathopen{}\left(rt+2\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (-6) = -6.

The number -6 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(-6)\amp -1(6)\amp 2(-3)\amp -2(3)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -1, and this sum happens with 2+(-3)=-1 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]r^{2}t^{2}-rt-6\amp =r^{2}t^{2}+2rt-3rt-6
ewline \amp =rt\mathopen{}\left(rt+2ight)-3\mathopen{}\left(rt+2ight)
ewline \amp =\left(rt-3ight)\mathopen{}\left(rt+2ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in r^{2}t^{2}-rt-6 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, -6, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(-6)\amp -1(6)\amp 2(-3)\amp -2(3)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with r^{2}t^{2}-rt-6 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}t^{2}-rt-6\amp =\left(rt-3\right)\mathopen{}\left(rt+2\right)\end{aligned}
